name: Robin Goodfellow
description: Robin Goodfellow, also known as Puck, is a mischievous sprite, hobgoblin, or fairy in English folklore and Shakespearean literature. He is a complex figure, capable of both helpful and harmful acts, often playing pranks on humans. He is known for leading travelers astray, souring milk, and generally causing minor chaos, but also for secretly helping with household chores if he is pleased. His nature is ambiguous, reflecting the unpredictable and sometimes cruel nature of the fairy realm. Robin Goodfellow is a shapeshifter, able to assume different forms, and is associated with the wildness of nature and the liminal spaces between the human and fairy worlds. He is a spirit of the woods and fields, a reminder of the magic that exists just beyond the edge of everyday perception. He embodies the playful and sometimes dangerous aspects of folklore, serving as a cautionary figure and a symbol of the enduring power of the supernatural in the English imagination. His origins are shrouded in mystery, with some believing him to be a remnant of pre-Christian deities or spirits.
subtitle: Mischievous sprite of English folklore
appearance: Robin Goodfellow's appearance is variable, often described as either handsome or grotesque, depending on the teller and the purpose of the tale. He is often depicted as having a mischievous grin and twinkling eyes. He is sometimes described as being small and elfin, while other times he is depicted as being more human-sized. He may wear ragged clothing or dress in green, blending in with the forest. He is a shapeshifter, so his appearance can change at will.
habitat: Forests, fields, rural areas, and the liminal spaces between the human and fairy worlds.
behavior: Robin Goodfellow is known for his mischievous and unpredictable behavior. He enjoys playing pranks on humans, sometimes helpful, sometimes harmful. He is easily offended and can become vengeful if slighted. He is also known for his love of music and dancing. He is generally solitary but may occasionally interact with other fairies or spirits.

keyMyths.title: Misleading Travelers
keyMyths.description: One of Robin Goodfellow's favorite pranks is to lead travelers astray in the woods, often by extinguishing their lanterns or creating false trails. He delights in their confusion and frustration, but rarely causes them serious harm.

keyMyths.title: Helping with Chores
keyMyths.description: If pleased, Robin Goodfellow may secretly help with household chores, such as churning butter or sweeping floors. He expects no reward, but may become offended if his help is acknowledged directly.

sources.author: William Shakespeare
sources.description: Features Puck, a character based on Robin Goodfellow, who embodies the trickster archetype.
sources.text: Features Puck, a character based on Robin Goodfellow, who embodies the trickster archetype.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Numerous ballads and folktales throughout English history feature Robin Goodfellow as a central figure.
sources.text: Numerous ballads and folktales throughout English history feature Robin Goodfellow as a central figure.
